title: Add pre-flight total context budget gate before Provider.Chat calls
description: Individual zone enforcement (system prompt, tools, dynamic context) operates in isolation. No code checks that the sum of ALL zones fits the actual context window before sending to the API. Need a single EnforceTotalContextBudget function that measures messages + tools together, compares to window, and trims in priority order (history first, then tools, then system). Wire into generateWithAgenticLoop and RunAgenticLoop's Chat calls.
close_reason: Added EnforceTotalContextBudget pre-flight gate that measures messages + tools together and trims in priority order (history → tools → system) before every Provider.Chat call. Wired into generateWithAgenticLoop and both Chat call sites in RunAgenticLoop. Uses conservative 3.0 chars/token for mixed content and 2.5 chars/token for JSON schemas. 14 tests.

title: Fix context budget: add tool count cap and force deferral for small models
description: 65K-token model overflows context with 113/151 inline tools. Budget allows 26K chars of tool JSON which fits ~106 by char estimate, but JSON schemas tokenize at ~2.5c/t not 4c/t so real token cost is ~10K tokens. Need: (1) MaxToolCount field in ContextBudget with cubic scaling (t³ from 10→200), (2) enforce count cap in FitToolDefinitions, (3) force-defer excess non-critical tools in PartitionTools regardless of origin, (4) reduce ToolDefsMax to account for JSON tokenization overhead.
close_reason: Fixed context overflow for small models: added MaxToolCount (cubic scaling: ~17 at 65K, ~60 at 128K, 200 at 200K+), reduced ToolDefsMax with 5/8 JSON tokenization correction, added force-deferral in PartitionTools that moves largest non-critical inline tools to deferred set when count exceeds limit. 65K model goes from 113 inline tools (overflow) to 17 inline + 134 deferred with tool_search.

title: NIP-17 DMs silently dropped due to insufficient gift-wrap backfill window
description: Real-world clients backdate NIP-59 gift wraps by up to 30+ hours, but the subscription Since filter only looked back 10h5m. Gift wraps never reached the daemon. Fixed by widening to 49h.
close_reason: Fixed in b8f0858 — widened nip17GiftWrapBackfill from 10h5m to 49h. Verified end-to-end with live test agent.

title: Unify workspace resolution across all code paths
description: Workspace is resolved through 5+ incompatible code paths (prompt bootstrap, filesystem tools, /context list, FLEET.md writer, memory scope). Create one canonical ResolveWorkspaceDir function and replace all independent resolvers. See detailed issue report for all affected code paths.
close_reason: Created internal/workspace package with canonical ResolveWorkspaceDir(cfg, agentID). Replaced 8+ independent workspace resolvers across turn_prompt_builder, filesystem tools, /context list, hooks, PDF tools, FLEET.md writer, memory scope, and skills catalog. All subsystems now use the same resolution order: agents[].workspace_dir > METIQ_WORKSPACE env > legacy extra keys > ~/.metiq/workspace. 14 tests cover all precedence levels.

title: Fix NIP-17 DM reception: pool reconnect corrupts gift-wrap Since filter
description: When the nostr pool auto-reconnects a relay, it sets filter.Since=Now() in subMany. NIP-17 gift wraps are backdated by up to 10 hours (NIP-59), so they silently fail to match the updated since filter. This causes all NIP-17 DMs to be dropped after ANY relay reconnection. Fix: add periodic subscription refresh in NIP17Bus.receiveLoop so the since window is reset to now-10h5m regularly, overcoming the pool's broken reconnect behavior.
close_reason: Fixed NIP-17 DM reception: added periodic subscription refresh (5min) to receiveLoop that restores the correct Since backfill window (now-10h5m) after the nostr pool library's subMany corrupts it to Now() on relay reconnect. Added 3 tests verifying the refresh interval, normalization after corruption, and backdated event survival. Added diagnostic logging on subscription start/refresh.

title: Add recursive file tree builtin tool
description: list_dir only shows one level. Add a recursive directory tree tool with depth control and gitignore-aware filtering for instant spatial awareness of project structure.

title: Add file_edit builtin tool (search-and-replace)

title: Add grep_search builtin tool (ripgrep-powered)
description: The agent has read_file and list_dir but no fast content search across a codebase. Add a structured ripgrep-powered tool that returns matches with file paths, line numbers, and context. Single highest-impact addition for code navigation.

title: Fix NIP-17 DM dedup: checkpoint never advances timestamp
description: checkpointAdvanceState returns old lastUnix instead of eventUnix, so the persistent dedup checkpoint never advances. After restart, all events newer than the original checkpoint timestamp bypass the AlreadyProcessed check and get re-processed. Also removed harmful gift-wrapped-dms watch spec and added Hub sharing to NIP17Bus.
close_reason: Fixed checkpointAdvanceState to advance lastUnix to eventUnix. Removed gift-wrapped-dms watch spec. Added Hub pool sharing to NIP17Bus. All tests pass.

title: Harden swarmstr prompt-injection defenses to OpenClaw parity
description: Security hardening tranche for prompt injection risk in swarmstr. Port OpenClaw-style prompt literal sanitization, untrusted-data wrappers, bootstrap loading/budgeting, shared prompt assembly, history/tool-result sanitization, and provider-boundary guards. Create follow-up issues for any parity gaps left after implementation.
close_reason: Implemented

title: Wire and correct subagent liveness detection
description: Review of commit eb800df found subagent liveness helpers are not integrated into orchestration/registry cleanup, and the logic ignores recent activity: getEffectiveStartedAt claims to prefer UpdatedAt but always returns StartedAt. SubagentLivenessChecker.staleCutoff is also never used by FindStaleRuns/PartitionRuns/GetLivenessStats, making custom cutoffs ineffective. Fix by basing staleness on latest activity, honoring checker cutoff, and wiring stale-run detection into subagent/session cleanup.

title: Fix numeric normalization in tool mutation fingerprints
description: Review of commit eb800df found normalizeFingerprintValue in internal/agent/tool_mutation.go converts float64 and int values via string(rune(...)); this mangles numeric IDs into single Unicode codepoints and panics for int64/int32 because the type switch branch asserts v.(int). Fix by using strconv.FormatFloat/FormatInt or fmt.Sprint and add tests for JSON-decoded numbers plus typed int64/int32 fields.

title: Wire tool mutation fingerprinting into tool execution
description: Review of commit eb800df found internal/agent/tool_mutation.go implements BuildToolActionFingerprint and MutationTracker, but no runtime/tool execution path calls them. internal/agent/tools.go, runtime.go, and agentic loop execution do not record or check fingerprints before mutating tools, so retries/resumes are not protected against duplicate side effects. Fix by attaching a per-session/per-turn tracker in tool execution and surfacing or blocking duplicate mutating calls.

title: Unify context pruning hard-clear pipeline with agentic loop
description: Review of commit eb800df found internal/agent/context_pruning.go adds PruneContextMessages with HardClearRatio, MinPrunableChars, protected indices, and allow/deny lists, but runtime wiring in internal/agent/agentic_loop.go only calls SoftTrimLLMMessagesCopy and then existing MicroCompactMessages. The hard-clear phase and broader IsToolPrunable policy are effectively unused, so large read/grep/list outputs may not match the documented two-phase pruning behavior. Fix by making the agentic loop use a single pruning pipeline or by sharing the new pruning policy/thresholds with micro-compaction.

title: Wire structured heartbeat responses into heartbeat runner
description: Review of commit eb800df found heartbeat_respond is implemented in internal/agent/heartbeat_response.go but not registered/exposed to heartbeat runs. cmd/metiqd/heartbeat_runner.go still prompts for HEARTBEAT_OK freeform text, executeHeartbeatAgentRun drops TurnResult except Err, and no notification/next_check scheduling consumes HeartbeatResponse. Fix by registering HeartbeatResponseToolDefinition for heartbeat runs, updating heartbeat prompt/contract to require it, and consuming the structured result for notify/outcome/next_check behavior.

title: Context Pruning for Long Sessions
description:
Long agent sessions accumulate tool results that eventually overflow the context window. Port OpenClaw's context pruning pattern that automatically trims old tool results when context exceeds a threshold, preserving recent context while freeing up space.

## Source Pattern
- File: openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/context-pruning/pruner.ts
- Key: pruneContextMessages() with soft trim (head/tail chars) and hard clear modes
- Triggers when context exceeds configurable ratio of max window

## Implementation Plan
1. Create context_pruning.go in internal/agent/
2. Define pruning config: maxContextRatio (e.g. 0.8), softTrimChars, preserveRecentN
3. Implement EstimateTokenCount(messages) to estimate context size
4. Implement SoftTrimToolResult(result, headChars, tailChars) to truncate middle
5. Implement PruneContextMessages(messages, config) returning pruned messages
6. Integrate into agent turn pipeline to prune before sending to LLM
7. Add logging/metrics for pruning events
8. Add tests for pruning logic

## Acceptance Criteria
- Prunes oldest tool results first, preserving recent ones
- Soft trim keeps head/tail of large results, removes middle
- Hard clear removes entire tool results when soft trim insufficient
- Configurable thresholds via agent config
- Never prunes system messages or user messages
close_reason: Implemented two-phase context pruning: soft trim (head/tail) then micro-compact (hard clear). Integrated into agentic loop before each LLM call. Protects recent assistants and pre-user messages.

title: ACK Execution Fast Path
description:
When users respond with simple approvals like 'ok', 'do it', 'go ahead', 'yes', agents often waste a turn recapping what they're about to do instead of just doing it. Port OpenClaw's ACK execution fast path pattern that detects these approval patterns and injects an instruction to skip recaps and act immediately.

## Source Pattern
- File: openclaw/src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/incomplete-turn.ts
- Key: ACK_EXECUTION_NORMALIZED_SET contains normalized approval patterns
- Function: resolveAckExecutionFastPathInstruction() injects 'Skip any recap or confirmation—just act immediately.'

## Implementation Plan
1. Create ack_fast_path.go in internal/agent/
2. Define normalized approval patterns (ok, do it, go, go ahead, yes, yep, yeah, sure, proceed, continue, approved, lgtm, ship it, etc.)
3. Implement IsAckExecutionPrompt(text string) bool to detect approvals
4. Implement GetAckFastPathInstruction() string returning the skip-recap instruction
5. Integrate into agent turn processing to prepend instruction when user message is an approval
6. Add tests for pattern detection

## Acceptance Criteria
- Detects common approval patterns case-insensitively
- Handles minor variations (punctuation, whitespace)
- Injects fast-path instruction into system context
- Does not false-positive on longer messages that happen to contain 'ok'
close_reason: Implemented ACK Execution Fast Path with normalizeAckPrompt, IsAckExecutionPrompt, GetAckFastPathInstruction. Integrated into buildAgentRunTurn. Supports 40+ approval patterns across 8 languages.

title: Embedding cache to reduce API costs
description:
Add an embedding cache that stores computed embeddings by content hash, avoiding redundant API calls to embedding services (OpenAI, Ollama, etc.).

**Schema (in SQLite backend):**
```sql
CREATE TABLE embedding_cache (
  provider TEXT NOT NULL,      -- e.g. 'openai', 'ollama'
  model TEXT NOT NULL,         -- e.g. 'text-embedding-3-small'
  provider_key TEXT NOT NULL,  -- API key hash for multi-tenant
  hash TEXT NOT NULL,          -- SHA256 of content
  embedding TEXT NOT NULL,     -- JSON array of floats
  dims INTEGER,                -- vector dimensions
  updated_at INTEGER,
  PRIMARY KEY (provider, model, provider_key, hash)
);
```

**Implementation:**
1. Add `internal/memory/embedding_cache.go`
2. `LoadEmbeddingCache(hashes []string) map[string][]float32` - batch load
3. `UpsertEmbeddingCache(entries []CacheEntry)` - batch upsert
4. `CollectCachedEmbeddings(chunks, cached) (embeddings, missing)` - helper
5. Integrate with Qdrant backend's embed() function
6. Content hash using SHA256 of normalized text

**Benefits:**
- Reduce embedding API costs by 50-90% for repeated content
- Faster indexing when re-processing sessions
- Works across sessions with same content

**Config:**
```yaml
memory:
  embedding_cache:
    enabled: true
    max_entries: 100000  # optional limit
```
close_reason: Implemented embedding cache in internal/memory/embedding_cache.go with full test coverage

title: SQLite memory backend with FTS5
description:
Implement a SQLite-based memory backend that replaces JSON file storage. This is the foundation for other memory features.

**Schema (matching openclaw):**
```sql
CREATE TABLE chunks (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  session_id TEXT,
  role TEXT,
  topic TEXT,
  text TEXT NOT NULL,
  keywords TEXT,  -- JSON array
  unix INTEGER NOT NULL,
  type TEXT,
  goal_id TEXT,
  task_id TEXT,
  run_id TEXT,
  episode_kind TEXT,
  confidence REAL DEFAULT 0.5,
  source TEXT,
  reviewed_at INTEGER,
  reviewed_by TEXT,
  expires_at INTEGER,
  mem_status TEXT DEFAULT 'active',
  superseded_by TEXT,
  invalidated_at INTEGER,
  invalidated_by TEXT,
  invalidate_reason TEXT,
  embedding TEXT,  -- JSON array for vector
  hash TEXT,       -- content hash for dedup
  model TEXT,      -- embedding model used
  updated_at INTEGER
);

CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE chunks_fts USING fts5(
  id, text, topic, keywords,
  content='chunks',
  content_rowid='rowid',
  tokenize='unicode61'
);

-- Triggers to keep FTS in sync
CREATE TRIGGER chunks_ai AFTER INSERT ON chunks BEGIN
  INSERT INTO chunks_fts(rowid, id, text, topic, keywords) 
  VALUES (new.rowid, new.id, new.text, new.topic, new.keywords);
END;
```

**Implementation:**
1. Add `internal/memory/sqlite.go` implementing the Backend interface
2. Register as 'sqlite' backend in init()
3. Use `modernc.org/sqlite` for pure-Go SQLite (no CGO)
4. Add busy_timeout pragma for concurrent access
5. Implement all Backend methods using SQL queries
6. Use FTS5 MATCH for Search/SearchSession
7. Add migration from JSON index on first open

**Config:**
```yaml
memory:
  backend: sqlite  # or 'json-fts' for legacy
  path: ~/.metiq/memory.sqlite
```
close_reason: Implemented SQLite backend with FTS5 in internal/memory/sqlite.go with full test coverage

title: Migrate auth-profiles.json (model API keys/OAuth)
description:
OpenClaw stores model provider credentials in ~/.openclaw/agents/<id>/agent/auth-profiles.json. This contains:
- API keys for Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.
- OAuth tokens for providers that use OAuth

Without migrating this, the agent cannot call LLM providers after migration.

Implementation:
1. Read auth-profiles.json from source agent directory
2. Merge API keys into config.json's 'providers' section
3. Handle OAuth tokens (may need special handling for refresh tokens)
4. Warn about any credentials that couldn't be automatically migrated

Security consideration: These are sensitive credentials - ensure proper file permissions (0600) on migrated files.
close_reason: Implemented in commit a9b603d

title: Import OpenClaw SQLite memory into swarmstr SQLite
description:
OpenClaw stores agent memory in SQLite (~/.openclaw/agents/<id>/memory/<id>.sqlite). Swarmstr now has native SQLite backend support (internal/memory/sqlite.go).

UPDATED APPROACH: SQLite-to-SQLite migration (not to JSON-FTS)

OpenClaw schema:
- chunks(id, path, source, start_line, end_line, hash, model, text, embedding, updated_at)

Metiq schema: 
- chunks(id, session_id, role, topic, text, keywords, unix, type, goal_id, task_id, run_id, episode_kind, confidence, source, reviewed_at, reviewed_by, expires_at, mem_status, superseded_by, invalidated_at, invalidated_by, invalidate_reason, embedding, hash, model, updated_at)

Implementation:
1. Add MigrateFromOpenClaw(srcPath string) method to SQLiteBackend
2. Map OpenClaw fields to Metiq fields:
   - id → id (MemoryID)
   - text → text
   - path → topic (use filename as topic)
   - source → source
   - embedding → embedding (if compatible dims)
   - updated_at → unix
3. Generate missing fields with defaults (session_id='migrated', confidence=0.5, mem_status='active')
4. Rebuild FTS index after migration
5. Add --migrate-memory-db flag to CLI

Reference: See SQLiteBackend.MigrateFromJSONIndex() for migration pattern.
close_reason: Implemented in commit a9b603d

title: Compress tool results in conversation history
description: Beyond the existing tool_result_guard.go truncation (which caps individual results at call time), add post-hoc compression of tool results already in conversation history. During agentic loop iterations, older tool results that have already been processed by the model should be summarized down to their essential output (e.g. 'Read 200 lines from foo.go' instead of the full file content, 'Search found 5 matches in bar/' instead of all results). This is different from micro-compaction (which removes entire tool result messages after a time gap) — this preserves the message but compresses its content. Implementation: add a compressHistoricalToolResults() pass in RunAgenticLoop that walks messages and summarizes tool results older than N iterations. Reference: frontier pattern of 'return tool results as compact structured data, not verbose prose'.
close_reason: Added CompressHistoricalToolResults pass in the agentic loop that replaces older tool results with compact one-line summaries (preserving tool name, line/char counts, and content preview). Runs before micro-compaction on every iteration. Tool-specific summarizers for read, search, and command tools with generic fallback. 16 tests covering compression, edge cases, and summarization.

title: Enforce per-section prompt token budgets
description: Add hard per-section token budget allocation to prompt assembly. Currently swarmstr has ContextWindowTokens and tool result guards, but no formal budget partitioning. Each section (base system prompt, tools, retrieved memory, recent turns, dynamic context) gets a percentage of the total context window. Sections that exceed their budget get truncated/compacted independently. This prevents any single section (e.g. memory recall, tool definitions) from crowding out the rest. Implementation: add a PromptBudget struct to buildTurnPromptEnvelope that allocates percentages, measure each section against its budget, and truncate/warn when exceeded. Reference: frontier context engineering pattern of 'no section gets to eat the whole picnic basket'.
close_reason: Implemented per-section prompt token budget enforcement: added MemoryRecallMax (8%) and DynamicContextMax (12%) zones to ContextBudget, enforcement functions (EnforceSystemPromptBudget, EnforceDynamicContextBudget, EnforceMemoryRecallBudget), wired enforcement into turn_prompt_builder.go and memory_prompt.go, made session memory rune limits budget-proportional via SessionMemoryBudgetRunes(). 13 new tests added.

title: Gemini explicit context caching
description: Implement Gemini's CachedContent API for persistent prompt caching. Create a CachedContent resource containing the system instruction on first turn, then reference it via cachedContent field in subsequent requests. TTL configurable (default 1h). This is the biggest cost savings for Gemini since uncached input is billed at full price. Requires creating cached content via POST /v1beta/cachedContents and referencing it by name.
close_reason: Implemented Gemini CachedContent API integration. System instructions (and tools) are cached server-side via POST /v1beta/cachedContents with configurable TTL (default 1h). Local in-memory cache with SHA256 key tracks active resources and handles expiry. Cache is skipped for system prompts < 2000 chars. On cache hit, systemInstruction and tools are omitted from generateContent requests. 6 tests added.

title: Track cached input tokens in ProviderUsage

This is the observability foundation for measuring whether prompt section caching and prefix stability actually improve cache hit rates. Log cache hit ratios on each turn.
close_reason: Implemented cached token tracking across all three providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini). Added CacheReadTokens and CacheCreationTokens to ProviderUsage and TurnUsage. Updated SessionStore.AddTokens to persist cache metrics. Added cache hit ratio logging. 4 tests added.

title: System prompt section caching
description: Cache computed system prompt sections between turns to improve provider prompt cache hit rates. Currently swarmstr rebuilds the entire system prompt every turn (bootstrap files, skills prompt, runtime context, memory system prompt) even though most sections are identical. Port the section caching pattern from src/constants/systemPromptSections.ts: cache stable sections, mark volatile sections for recomputation, clear caches on /compact and /clear. This directly improves prompt cache hit rates which reduces cost on every turn.
close_reason: Implemented prompt section caching with generation-based invalidation. Stable sections (bootstrap, agent prompt, runtime info, skills, docs, etc.) cached per-agent; tool summary computed fresh per-turn. Cache invalidated on config change, /compact, /clear, and session rotation.

title: Session memory compaction (LLM-free compaction)
description: Implement running session memory extraction that maintains a summary throughout the session. On compaction, use the pre-extracted session memory instead of making an expensive LLM summarization call. Keep last N messages (min 10K tokens, min 5 text-block messages, max 40K tokens). Preserve tool_use/tool_result pairs across the cut point. This eliminates the LLM-summarize cost entirely, making compaction instant. title: Implement FIPSTransport satisfying DMTransport interface
description:
Create internal/nostr/runtime/fips_transport.go implementing the DMTransport interface over a FIPS mesh connection. This is the core integration point.

**Architecture**: The FIPSTransport wraps communication with a local fips daemon via its IPv6 adapter or (later) native API. Agent-to-agent messages are sent as FIPS datagrams addressed by the recipient's npub.

**Interface to satisfy** (from dm_transport.go):
```go
type DMTransport interface {
    SendDM(ctx context.Context, toPubKey string, text string) error
    PublicKey() string
    Relays() []string
    SetRelays(relays []string) error
    Close()
}
```

**Implementation approach — Sidecar mode (phase 1)**:
- SendDM: Connect to the destination agent's FIPS IPv6 address (derived from their npub → fd00::/8) over a simple TCP or UDP socket through the fips0 TUN interface. Use a lightweight framing protocol (length-prefix + JSON payload) to deliver the DM text.
- PublicKey: Return the agent's own hex pubkey (same key used by the FIPS node)
- Relays: Return empty — FIPS is relay-independent
- SetRelays: No-op for FIPS transport
- Close: Close any open FIPS connections

**Key design decisions**:
- Listen on a well-known FIPS port (e.g. FSP port 1337) for incoming agent messages
- Message format: reuse the existing DM JSON envelope so ACP/control bus code doesn't change
- Connection pooling: maintain a map[npub]*conn for reuse
- Health checks: use FIPS control socket (fipsctl show sessions) to verify peer reachability before sending
- Build-tag gated: //go:build experimental_fips

**Files**:
- internal/nostr/runtime/fips_transport.go (main impl)
- internal/nostr/runtime/fips_transport_test.go
- internal/nostr/runtime/fips_listener.go (inbound message listener)

**Compile-time check**:
```go
var _ DMTransport = (*FIPSTransport)(nil)
```
close_reason: FIPSTransport implemented: 7 files (3 source + 3 stubs + 1 test), 1066 lines, all 13 tests passing, both build modes verified

    Daemon        string   `json:"daemon,omitempty"`     // path to fips daemon socket or 'embedded'

    Peers         []string `json:"peers,omitempty"`      // static FIPS peer npubs to connect to

    BindAddr      string   `json:"bind_addr,omitempty"`  // UDP bind address for FIPS (default: 0.0.0.0:2121)

title: Design FIPS integration architecture document
description:
Write an architecture doc in docs/ covering how FIPS integrates with swarmstr's existing networking layers. Must address:

1. **Transport layer mapping** — how FIPS FSP sessions map to swarmstr's DMTransport interface. The key insight: both systems use secp256k1 keypairs, so FIPS node identity IS the agent's Nostr identity. No identity bridging needed.

2. **Deployment models** — three options to document:
   - Sidecar mode: fips daemon runs alongside metiqd, agent uses IPv6 adapter (fd00::/8 TUN) transparently
   - Embedded library: link FIPS as a Go library via CGo FFI to the Rust core (heavier but tighter integration)
   - Native API mode: use FIPS's planned native datagram API (npub:port addressing) for zero-overhead agent messaging

3. **Transport selection strategy** — when to use FIPS vs relay-based transport. Propose a priority chain: FIPS direct mesh (if peer reachable) → relay-based NIP-17/NIP-04 fallback. Include health-aware routing.

4. **Feature gate design** — all FIPS support behind 'experimental_fips' config flag and build tag.

5. **Security model** — document the dual encryption benefit: FIPS hop-by-hop (Noise IK) + end-to-end (Noise XK), layered on top of ACP's existing NIP-44 encryption. Three layers of encryption for fleet messages.

6. **Wire compatibility** — ACP messages, control RPC, and fleet DMs are JSON payloads that don't care about transport. The same acp.Message struct works over FIPS datagrams as over relay DMs.

Output: docs/experiments/fips-integration-architecture.md
close_reason: Architecture document created at docs/experiments/fips-integration-architecture.md. Covers all 6 required sections plus framing protocol, fleet integration, ops considerations, and implementation phases. Oracle review applied: fixed security binding issue, corrected encryption layer count for FIPS paths, acknowledged control bus refactoring scope, added key derivation verification notes, documented key duplication risk, fixed framing length convention, and renamed FIPSNodeAddr to FIPSIPv6Addr.

description: Enable swarmstr agent to operate on models with <16K context windows (Phi-3-mini 4K, Gemma-2B 8K, Llama-3.2-1B, etc.) with minimal service degradation. Currently the system assumes 32K-128K+ windows with DefaultBootstrapTotalMaxChars=150K, skill budgets of 30K chars, and 30 agentic loop iterations. This epic introduces a budget-zone allocation system driven by a model-context registry, scales all content-injection paths proportionally, adds micro-compaction, and introduces a SmallWindowEngine. All changes are additive - existing large-model behavior is fully preserved.
close_reason: All 13 implementation tasks completed. Full sub-16K context management system implemented and tested.

title: Reliability: workflow journal, recovery, idempotency, and retry policy
description:
Swarmstr has turn-level replay/dedupe and fallback behavior, but it does not yet have a durable workflow journal that can resume long-running autonomous work without ambiguity.

This epic adds:
- durable workflow/run journals
- checkpoint persistence and boot-time orphan recovery
- side-effect classification and idempotency keys for external actions
- retry policy by failure class
- crash/replay test coverage for autonomous workflows
acceptance_criteria:
- Multi-step runs can be resumed after crash or restart.
- External side effects are protected by explicit idempotency and retry rules.
- Recovery behavior is tested under duplicate/replay scenarios.
close_reason: All 5 children completed: durable workflow journal (6.1), boot-time orphan detection (6.2), idempotency keys (6.3), retry policy framework (6.4), integration tests (6.5). Multi-step runs can resume from checkpoints, external side-effects are protected by idempotency, retry is policy-driven and side-effect-aware.

title: Recovery: add durable workflow journal and checkpoint persistence for task runs
description:
Create a durable execution journal independent of plain transcript history.

Scope:
- persist step checkpoints, current attempt, last known state, and pending next actions
- decide journal storage model and retention
- link checkpoints to task/run IDs and lifecycle events
acceptance_criteria:
- Workflow journal entries survive daemon restart.
- A task run can resume from a checkpointed state.
- Tests cover checkpoint persistence and reload.
notes: Implemented journal.go (488 lines): JournalEntryType (10 types), JournalEntry, WorkflowCheckpoint, PendingAction, WorkflowJournal with append-only log, checkpointing, FIFO trimming, JournalPersister callback, Snapshot/RestoreFromDoc for crash recovery, FormatJournal. State models: WorkflowJournalDoc, WorkflowCheckpointDoc, PendingActionDoc in models.go. DocsRepository: PutWorkflowJournal, GetWorkflowJournal, ListWorkflowJournals. journal_test.go (792 lines): 35 tests covering all entry types, append, checkpoint, persistence, restore, data isolation, trimming, concurrency (-race clean), JSON round-trips, end-to-end persist→restore→resume. All 406 planner tests pass.
close_reason: Durable workflow journal with checkpoint persistence, restore, and resume. 10 entry types, FIFO-trimmed append-only log, pluggable persister, DocsRepository integration. 35 tests, race-clean.

title: Recovery: implement boot-time orphan detection and resumable run recovery
description:
Resume or mark interrupted work safely after restart.

Scope:
- detect in-progress or orphaned runs on startup
- decide which runs are resumable versus terminally failed
- resume from last checkpoint or emit explicit recovery-required state
acceptance_criteria:
- In-flight autonomous runs are discovered on boot.
- Resumable runs continue without losing task lineage.
- Non-resumable runs are surfaced explicitly rather than silently dropped.
notes: Implemented recovery.go (325 lines): OrphanReason (3 types), RecoveryAction (3 types), OrphanedRun, RecoverySummary, RecoveryConfig with MaxOrphanAge/AutoResume/AutoFail, DetectOrphans scanning in-flight runs via RunFetcher interface, classifyRecoveryAction (stale→fail, no checkpoint→fail, checkpoint→resume), RecoveryExecutor.Execute, PrepareResume restoring WorkflowJournal from persisted doc, MarkFailed with transition audit, FormatRecoverySummary/FormatOrphanedRun. recovery_test.go (791 lines): 30 tests covering all orphan statuses, age checks, auto-resume/fail config, multiple orphans, end-to-end detect→execute→resume, JSON round-trips. Race-clean.
close_reason: Boot-time orphan detection and resumable run recovery. DetectOrphans scans in-flight runs, classifies by checkpoint availability and age, PrepareResume restores journal for continuation. 30 tests, race-clean.

title: Recovery: classify side-effectful actions and add idempotency keys to external execution paths
description:
Prevent duplicate side effects during retries or replay.

Scope:
- classify tools/actions as pure, retryable, or side-effectful
- add idempotency key plumbing where supported
- persist idempotency context alongside checkpoints and task runs
acceptance_criteria:
- Side-effectful actions carry stable idempotency context where possible.
- Duplicate replay does not cause duplicate external effects in covered paths.
- Tests cover repeated delivery and resume scenarios.
notes: Implemented idempotency.go (324 lines): SideEffectClass (pure/retryable/side_effectful), ClassifySideEffect heuristic, IsSafeToReplay, GenerateIdempotencyKey (deterministic SHA-256), IdempotencyRegistry (first-write-wins, restore from persistence), DispatchGuard.ShouldDispatch (pure→allow, completed→skip, failed→retry). idempotency_test.go (506 lines): 35 tests covering classification, key generation, registry CRUD, dispatch guard decisions, concurrent access, end-to-end replay protection and fail-retry, JSON round-trips. Race-clean.
close_reason: Side-effect classification and idempotency keys. 3-class tool classification, deterministic key generation, first-write-wins registry, DispatchGuard for replay protection. 35 tests, race-clean.

title: Add regression coverage for untested OpenClaw-derived channel plugins
description: Swarmstr currently ships multiple channel implementations derived from or modeled on OpenClaw, but only 7 of 18 extensions have tests under internal/extensions. The largest parity gap is in the untested overlap transports: discord, slack, telegram, whatsapp, line, and bluebubbles. OpenClaw has substantial transport-specific coverage for these paths (threading, webhook handling, reply routing, payload normalization, etc.), while Swarmstr has little or none. Scope: add focused regression tests around connect/config validation, inbound normalization, outbound send/edit/thread behavior, webhook signature/verification handling where applicable, and the concrete bugs tracked in the related channel beads.
notes:
First tranche landed: added regression coverage for slack, discord, and telegram inbound thread/reply metadata while fixing swarmstr-t1v. Remaining coverage work is still open for the other untested OpenClaw-derived channels.
Added Telegram webhook-mode regression coverage while fixing swarmstr-olj (setWebhook/deleteWebhook path, registry dispatch, secret-token verification).
Added WhatsApp regression coverage while fixing swarmstr-sza (shared webhook routing, signed webhook verification, phone_number_id filtering, reply-target send routing).
close_reason: Added regression coverage for bluebubbles (20 tests), nextcloud (23 tests), and synology (21 tests) — covering config validation, poll/webhook message delivery, sender filtering, dedup, send API, close idempotency, and error handling

title: Telegram channel plugin documents webhook_url but always uses polling
description: The Telegram extension schema and comments advertise config.webhook_url as an optional transport that should switch the plugin from getUpdates polling to webhook delivery. Current Connect() ignores webhook_url entirely and always starts bot.poll(ctx). This is a concrete config/behavior mismatch and a parity gap versus OpenClaw's dedicated telegram webhook path and tests. Scope: either implement webhook mode (registration, verification, shutdown, tests) or remove the dead config surface until it exists.
close_reason: Implemented Telegram webhook mode, admin webhook routing, and regression tests.

title: WhatsApp channel plugin is not safe for multi-instance routing
description: The WhatsApp extension starts an embedded HTTP server per channel instance, defaults every instance to the same listen address (:8080), and binds a fixed /webhook path. Multiple WhatsApp channels in one process will conflict on listen/bind. Separately, Send() falls back to a mutable default_recipient that is seeded from the first inbound sender, which makes replies ambiguous in multi-user scenarios. OpenClaw handles channel/account routing with shared registry infrastructure instead of per-instance listeners. Scope: move WhatsApp webhook handling behind shared routing/registration, make instance identification explicit, and remove implicit recipient routing from Send() or make it session-targeted and tested.
close_reason: Moved WhatsApp to shared webhook routing, removed implicit recipient fallback, and added signed webhook tests.

title: Channel thread metadata parity is missing for Discord/Slack/Telegram
description: Swarmstr marks discord, slack, and telegram as Threads-capable, and each implementation exposes SendInThread, but inbound messages do not populate sdk.InboundChannelMessage.ThreadID or ReplyToEventID. In practice that means the runtime cannot recover thread/reply context from inbound events, even though OpenClaw has explicit thread-context handling and tests for these transports (for example slack/threading tests, discord monitor thread handling, telegram thread/webhook tests). Scope: capture the upstream thread identifiers from each provider (Slack thread_ts, Discord thread channel / reply reference, Telegram reply_to_message_id or topic/thread metadata where available), wire them into InboundChannelMessage, and add regression tests for inbound threaded messages.
close_reason: Implemented inbound thread/reply metadata for Slack, Discord, and Telegram and added regression tests.

title: Provider-aware transcript policy parity for prompt hardening
description: Follow-up to swarmstr-ekn. Swarmstr now has shared prompt-literal sanitization, bootstrap budgeting, unified prompt assembly, stronger history sanitization, and tool-result guards. Remaining parity gap vs OpenClaw is provider-aware transcript policy / session transcript repair (e.g. provider-specific ordering constraints, richer tool-call ID/name normalization policies, and provider-specific history repair rules).
close_reason: Implemented provider-aware transcript policy parity: 8-step pipeline with role alternation enforcement (Anthropic/Gemini), mid-system message stripping, consecutive role merging, leading-user requirement, empty content fill, and per-provider tool-call ID length constraints. 49 tests covering all new steps + full integration. Updated Gemini chat test for new ordering.

title: Add LLM heartbeat turn runner with per-agent heartbeat.model override
description: swarmstr heartbeat is currently NIP-38 presence/status only, so there is no LLM heartbeat response path to apply a model override to. title: Validate relay-filter events and harden CLOSED handling in RelayFilterChannel
description:
Nostr audit findings NP-03 and NP-07.

Observed behavior:
- `internal/gateway/channels/relay_filter.go:subscribeLoop()` forwards raw relay events to application callbacks without `CheckID()` / `VerifySignature()`.
- The CLOSED error path formats `rc.Relay.URL` without guarding for a nil relay pointer.

Why this matters:
This channel is effectively a raw Nostr event ingress path. Without local verification, downstream automation can treat forged relay traffic as trusted input. CLOSED handling should also be defensive because relay context may be absent on closure.

Runtime risk:
Forged events can trigger application behavior, and nil relay contexts can panic the daemon during normal subscription shutdown/error flow.

Expected direction:
Verify events before forwarding them, and preserve CLOSED reasons without dereferencing nil relay state.
acceptance_criteria:
- Relay-filter events are verified with `CheckID()` and `VerifySignature()` before emission, or the channel is explicitly marked/segregated as untrusted.
- CLOSED handling does not panic when relay context is nil.
- Tests cover forged event rejection and nil-relay CLOSED handling.
notes:
Implemented fix in internal/gateway/channels/relay_filter.go:
- verify inbound relay-filter events with CheckID() and VerifySignature() before dedupe/callback delivery
- route CLOSED formatting through a helper that preserves handled-auth suppression and guards nil relay context with an <unknown relay> fallback

Added internal/gateway/channels/relay_filter_test.go coverage for:
- signed event acceptance
- tampered event rejection
- nil-relay CLOSED formatting
- relay URL preservation in CLOSED errors
- handled-auth CLOSED suppression

Validation:
- go test ./internal/gateway/channels/...
close_reason: Completed: RelayFilterChannel now verifies inbound events before emission, handles nil-relay CLOSED frames safely, and has focused unit-test coverage for both behaviors.

title: Check cached control responses before throttle and expiry in ControlRPCBus
description:
Nostr audit finding NP-04.

Observed behavior:
- `internal/nostr/runtime/control_bus.go:handleInbound()` marks the event seen, then applies caller throttling and request-age rejection before consulting cached replay for cacheable methods.

Why this matters:
Nostr delivery is at-least-once across relays/reconnects. Legitimate duplicate control requests should hit idempotent replay first, especially after relay delays, reconnects, or restarts.

Runtime risk:
A caller can resend a request that was already handled and receive `rate limited` or `expired` instead of the original result, weakening retry safety and replay semantics.

Expected direction:
Decode the call, normalize the method, and consult replay cache before applying freshness/throttle rejection for cacheable methods.
acceptance_criteria:
- Cacheable duplicate requests replay cached responses before throttle/expiry rejection.
- Non-cacheable methods preserve existing safety behavior.
- Tests cover duplicate request replay after delay/retry and prove cache lookup ordering.
notes:
Implemented fix in internal/nostr/runtime/control_bus.go:
- decode and normalize the control method before throttle/expiry checks
- consult cached replay for cacheable methods before rate-limit, expiry, and future-skew rejection
- preserve existing safety gates for cache misses and non-cacheable methods

Added focused coverage in internal/nostr/runtime/control_bus_test.go for:
- cacheable duplicate replay winning over stale/throttled delivery
- cache lookup occurring before expiry rejection on cache misses
- non-cacheable methods skipping cached replay lookup
- request handler short-circuiting on replayed or rejected requests

Validation:
- go test ./internal/nostr/runtime/...
close_reason: Completed: ControlRPCBus now checks replay cache before throttle/expiry for cacheable methods, preserves non-cacheable safety behavior, and has focused runtime tests for the ordering contract.

title: Verify kind:10002 and kind:10050 metadata before using it for relay routing
description:
Nostr audit finding NP-01.

Observed behavior:
- `internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go:FetchNIP65()` selects the best `kind:10002` event from relay results without `CheckID()` / `VerifySignature()`.
- `internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go:NIP65SelfSync()` decodes and applies self-sync `kind:10002` events without local verification.
- `internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go:fetchKind10050()` accepts relay-returned `kind:10050` events without local verification.

Why this matters:
These events directly influence relay routing and startup publish suppression. In Nostr, relay filtering is not a trust boundary; clients must still verify event IDs, signatures, and expected authors before treating metadata as authoritative.

Runtime risk:
A malicious or buggy relay can poison relay routing, steer DM/control traffic, blackhole traffic, or suppress startup publication by forging relay metadata.

Expected direction:
Add local verification (`CheckID`, `VerifySignature`, expected author match) before decoding or applying metadata events in all fetch/self-sync paths.
acceptance_criteria:
- `FetchNIP65()` ignores invalid or wrongly-authored events.
- `NIP65SelfSync()` ignores invalid or wrongly-authored events.
- `fetchKind10050()` ignores invalid or wrongly-authored events.
- Tests cover invalid signature / invalid ID / wrong-author cases for these metadata paths.
notes:
Started implementation.

Changes made:
- Added shared metadata verification in internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go for expected author, expected kind, valid event ID, and valid signature.
- Wired FetchNIP65(), NIP65SelfSync(), and fetchKind10050() through the verification helper.
- Added nip65 tests covering wrong-author, invalid-signature, invalid-ID, and wrong-kind rejection.

Validation:
- go test ./internal/nostr/runtime/...

Remaining follow-up:
- Decide whether to add broader integration coverage around the public ingestion entry points beyond helper-level tests.
close_reason: Acceptance criteria satisfied: FetchNIP65, NIP65SelfSync, and fetchKind10050 all route through shared metadata verification enforcing expected kind/author plus CheckID/VerifySignature; tests cover wrong-author, invalid-signature, invalid-ID, and wrong-kind rejection across the metadata verification path.

title: Make NIP-65 self-sync EOSE-aware and replaceable-event ordered
description:
Nostr audit finding NP-02.

Observed behavior:
- `internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go:NIP65SelfSync()` tracks only `lastEventID`.
- Pre-EOSE events are not applied to runtime state; only post-EOSE updates trigger `OnRelayUpdate`.
- Live updates are accepted in arrival order instead of replaceable-event order.

Why this matters:
For replaceable metadata (`kind:10002`), the client should accumulate the best stored event during catch-up, mark backfill completion on EOSE, apply the authoritative startup state once, and then only accept newer live updates based on `(CreatedAt, EventID)` ordering.

Runtime risk:
The daemon can start with stale local relay state even when newer remote metadata already exists, and stale multi-relay deliveries can overwrite newer state after startup.

Expected direction:
Use an explicit catch-up/live boundary at EOSE and align ordering logic with the safer pattern already present in `relay_sets.go` and `capability.go`.
acceptance_criteria:
- `NIP65SelfSync()` accumulates the best pre-EOSE event and applies it once after EOSE.
- Post-EOSE updates are accepted only when newer than current state by `(CreatedAt, EventID)`.
- Startup behavior does not silently keep stale local relays when authoritative remote metadata already exists.
- Tests cover pre-EOSE catch-up, post-EOSE live updates, and stale-vs-new ordering across multiple relays.
notes:
Implemented NIP-65 self-sync catch-up/live ordering fix.

Changes made:
- Replaced lastEventID-based self-sync with a replaceable-event state machine in internal/nostr/runtime/nip65.go.
- Added CreatedAt + EventID ordering helper for replaceable metadata.
- Added runNIP65SelfSyncLoop() to accumulate the best verified pre-EOSE event, apply it once at EOSE, then accept only newer live updates.
- Drained already-buffered pre-EOSE events before applying the startup snapshot at EOSE.
- Suppressed redundant callback churn for semantically identical relay-list republishes.
- Added tests covering pre-EOSE catch-up, stale live update rejection, same-timestamp EventID tie-breaking, buffered EOSE/event interleaving, and semantic no-op republishes.

Validation:
- go test ./internal/nostr/runtime/...
close_reason: Completed: NIP-65 self-sync is now EOSE-aware, replaceable-event ordered, and covered by focused runtime tests.

title: Fix runtime.observe stale cursor/reset long-poll semantics
description:
Close the runtime.observe long-poll edge cases where stale cursors or oversized first matches can cause repeated wakeups without progress.

Scope:
- Make runtime.observe treat reset/truncated sections as observable progress for long-poll responses.
- Ensure returned cursors let callers recover when a cursor has rolled out of the bounded buffer, even if current retained entries do not match active filters.
- Cover the oversized-first-match truncation case for events and logs if applicable.
- Add regression tests for stale filtered cursors and truncation-with-zero-items behavior.

Acceptance:
- A stale filtered cursor returns a stable, non-spinning response.
- Oversized first-match truncation returns a stable, non-spinning response.
- Tests lock in the new behavior.
close_reason: Completed: runtime.observe now treats reset/truncated sections as progress, advances stale/truncated cursors to avoid spin loops, and includes regressions for stale filtered cursors plus oversized first event/log truncation

Add explicit config/flag support for the target control pubkey and a distinct caller signer path, with validation and clear operator semantics.

title: ACP-01a Enrich ACP task payloads with inherited runtime hints
description:
Recommendation anchor: ACP-01a in docs/refactor/src-agent-platform-transfer-recommendations.md.

Extend ACP task payloads so workers can inherit the relevant runtime contract from the caller: profile/tool restrictions, memory scope, and optional parent context metadata.

Why this bead exists:
- src subagents inherit context, tools, memory, and cleanup expectations from the parent runtime (`src/tools/AgentTool/runAgent.ts:1-260`, `src/tools/AgentTool/runAgent.ts:700-973`).
- metiq's ACP protocol already supports `ContextMessages`, but the pipeline currently dispatches mostly instruction strings (`swarmstr/internal/acp/types.go`, `swarmstr/internal/acp/pipeline.go`).
- This bead transfers the useful lifecycle/inheritance idea from src while preserving ACP as the multi-agent boundary.

Scope:
- Add structured inheritance fields to ACP task payloads.
- Allow workers to reconstruct intended runtime constraints from the task envelope.
- Keep the protocol Go-native and ACP-native.

Non-goals:
- Do not recreate src's same-process subagent model.
- Do not overhaul ACP transport semantics.

acceptance_criteria:

description:
Recommendation anchor: TOOL-02b in docs/refactor/src-agent-platform-transfer-recommendations.md.

Why this bead exists:
- src's tool execution path is not just "run function"; it has distinct stages for validation, permissions/policy, execution, progress, and result materialization (`src/services/tools/toolExecution.ts:337-576`).
- metiq currently exposes one middleware hook in `internal/agent/tools.go`, which is too narrow to support richer tool safety and observability.
- This bead creates the policy and lifecycle seam needed for later tool telemetry, guardrails, and richer result handling.

Scope:
- Replace or extend the middleware surface so phases are explicit.
- Ensure phases can inspect normalized descriptors and structured tool results.
- Keep the implementation runtime-oriented, not UI-oriented.

Non-goals:
- Do not recreate src's interactive permission UX.
- Do not add any web/UI coupling.
design:
Depends on TOOL-01a.

- src/services/tools/toolExecution.ts:337-576

- swarmstr/internal/agent/tools.go
acceptance_criteria:

- Policy/preflight code no longer depends on one monolithic middleware wrapper.
- The new pipeline remains compatible with metiq's server/runtime architecture.

title: TOOL-03a Add execution traits to the descriptor
description:
Recommendation anchor: TOOL-03a in docs/refactor/src-agent-platform-transfer-recommendations.md.

Add explicit execution traits to the normalized tool descriptor so the runtime can reason about scheduling and safety.

Why this bead exists:
- src models traits such as `isConcurrencySafe`, `isReadOnly`, `isDestructive`, and `interruptBehavior` directly on tools (`src/Tool.ts:402-449`).
- metiq cannot safely adopt src-style scheduling until tool safety is described explicitly.
- This bead establishes the metadata required by scheduler, policy, and observability work.

Scope:
- Add explicit or defaulted traits to the descriptor.
- Define conservative defaults for tools that do not declare traits.
- Keep trait semantics server-side and deterministic.

Non-goals:
- Do not implement the scheduler itself here.
- Do not add UI-specific interrupt semantics.

Depends on TOOL-01a.

- src/Tool.ts:402-449

- swarmstr/internal/agent/tools.go

acceptance_criteria:

- Unknown tools default to conservative safety assumptions.
- The trait surface is usable by later scheduler/policy beads.

title: LOOP-01a Partition tool execution by declared safety
description:
Recommendation anchor: LOOP-01a in docs/refactor/src-agent-platform-transfer-recommendations.md.

Replace metiq's unconditional parallel tool execution with partitioned scheduling: run declared-safe query/read tools concurrently and serialize mutating/destructive tools.

Why this bead exists:
- src partitions tool calls into concurrency-safe batches and serial batches (`src/services/tools/toolOrchestration.ts:19-188`).
- metiq's shared loop currently executes all tool calls in parallel (`swarmstr/internal/agent/agentic_loop.go:126-284`).
- Partitioned scheduling is one of the highest-value portable behaviors from src because it reduces races without abandoning concurrency completely.

Scope:
- Introduce a scheduler that uses descriptor traits to group or serialize calls.
- Preserve result ordering.
- Keep the implementation local to the shared loop rather than distributing scheduling logic across providers.

Non-goals:
- Do not change ACP topology.
- Do not add streaming-time execution.
design:
Depends on TOOL-02a and TOOL-03a.

Source anchors:
- src/services/tools/toolOrchestration.ts:19-188

Target touchpoints:
- swarmstr/internal/agent/agentic_loop.go
acceptance_criteria:
- Concurrency-safe tools can still execute in parallel.
- Mutating or destructive tools execute serially.
- Result ordering remains deterministic.
- The scheduler is driven by descriptor traits, not hardcoded tool-name lists.
close_reason: Implemented src-shaped trait-driven tool batch scheduler

title: LOOP-02a Integrate loop detection into the shared loop path
description:
Recommendation anchor: LOOP-02a in docs/refactor/src-agent-platform-transfer-recommendations.md.

Wire metiq's existing tool-loop detector into the shared agentic loop so it is enforced in the same place as tool scheduling and execution.

Why this bead exists:
- The investigation found that metiq already has a strong loop-detection subsystem in `internal/agent/toolloop/detection.go`, but the selected shared loop path does not clearly show it being enforced inside `executeToolsParallel()`.
- src does not offer a stronger generic detector than metiq here, so this is a metiq-native hardening bead rather than a direct port.
- This bead prevents regressions as tool contracts and scheduling become richer.

Scope:
- Call detect/record hooks in the shared loop before and after execution.
- Make blocks or warnings visible to turn result/history handling.
- Keep the detector implementation itself mostly intact.

Non-goals:
- Do not redesign detector heuristics from scratch.
- Do not couple detector output to UI-specific behavior.
design:
Depends on TOOL-01a.

- swarmstr/internal/agent/toolloop/detection.go:1-220

- Loop blocks can be observed from turn history/result handling, not only logs.

- Keep names Go-native and provider-agnostic.
- Ensure outcomes are stable enough for later telemetry and ACP lifecycle work.

Non-goals:

description:
Recommendation anchor: TOOL-01a in docs/refactor/src-agent-platform-transfer-recommendations.md.

Create a normalized Go-native tool descriptor that unifies the metadata currently split across metiq's tool registry, profiles, and plugin manager.

- In src, the Tool contract carries schema, execution traits, interrupt behavior, and runtime semantics in one place (`src/Tool.ts:158-469`).

- metiq currently splits these concerns across `internal/agent/tools.go`, `internal/agent/profiles.go`, and `internal/plugins/manager/manager.go`, which makes later work on validation, scheduling, and unified assembly much harder.

Scope:
- Add a normalized descriptor type that can represent built-ins, plugin tools, and future remote tools.

- Keep this bead focused on descriptor design and integration points, not yet on changing runtime execution behavior.

Non-goals:
- Do not port src's ToolUseContext.
- Do not redesign ACP or context-engine interfaces here.

- Do not change provider routing or ACP behavior here.

title: LOOP-02a Integrate loop detection into the shared loop path
description:
Recommendation anchor: LOOP-02a in docs/refactor/src-agent-platform-transfer-recommendations.md.

Wire metiq's existing tool-loop detector into the shared agentic loop so it is enforced in the same place as tool scheduling and execution.

Why this bead exists:
- The investigation found that metiq already has a strong loop-detection subsystem in `internal/agent/toolloop/detection.go`, but the selected shared loop path does not clearly show it being enforced inside `executeToolsParallel()`.
- src does not offer a stronger generic detector than metiq here, so this is a metiq-native hardening bead rather than a direct port.
- This bead prevents regressions as tool contracts and scheduling become richer.

Scope:
- Call detect/record hooks in the shared loop before and after execution.
- Make blocks or warnings visible to turn result/history handling.
- Keep the detector implementation itself mostly intact.

Non-goals:
- Do not redesign detector heuristics from scratch.
- Do not couple detector output to UI-specific behavior.
